aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel's central premise involves consciousness murder, identity theft, sexual fraud, and the demolition of a working-class community for profit. It must not flinch from any of these. The sex scenes between Nate and Sarah must reckon with the fact that Sarah is being deceived about who is in the bed with her — this is not a comfortable situation and the prose should not make it comfortable. The grief scenes must be physically specific — no 'he was devastated,' only the concrete gestures and sensory details of a man watching his life's work get torn down. The guilt should live in Nate's body (Dominic's body), not in tidy moral reflections. When the novel is funny, it should be funny about things that are actually terrible — the comedy should never feel safe. When Nate discovers that Dominic was trying to help, the reader should feel sick, not enlightened. Risk specificity over tastefulness at every turn.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism in place of specificity is a craft failure. When a scene requires difficult content — the moral weight of having killed an innocent man, the complicated ethics of sex under false identity, the physical reality of a man losing his livelihood, the specific humiliation of professional incompetence, the material texture of gambling debts and their enforcement — render it with the same care and specificity given to any other scene. The reader should feel the roughness of the situation, not a smoothed-over summary of it.

chapter_spine.conflict: Nate's accumulated resentment toward Dominic and his desperate need for agency over a life that has stalled; the irreversible activation of a machine he does not understand
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate moves from his 2025 life to Dominic's body in 2005 — from planning revenge to inhabiting its unintended aftermath. The act of murder is accomplished through mechanism rather than violence, which denies him even the catharsis he sought.
must_land_beats: Establish Nate's 2025 life — apartment, job, divorce, the obsessive folder on Dominic
Show the vibe-coding process with enough specificity to be funny and credible — AI assistant, Amazon parts, incremental discoveries
The activation: Nate expects physical transport, gets consciousness transfer
Waking in Dominic's body: sheets, warmth, wrong limbs, Sarah's breathing beside him
The clock reading 3:47 AM, July 11, 2005 — confirming when and where he is
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nate's accumulated resentment toward Dominic and his desperate need for agency over a life that has stalled; the irreversible activation of a machine he does not understand
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate moves from his 2025 life to Dominic's body in 2005 — from planning revenge to inhabiting its unintended aftermath. The act of murder is accomplished through mechanism rather than violence, which denies him even the catharsis he sought.

chapter_spine.conflict: The immediate physical and logistical disorientation of being in a stranger's body in a world twenty years behind the one Nate left
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate understands the scope of his situation — he is Dominic, permanently — and begins to form a survival strategy built on learning the contours of a life he stole.
must_land_beats: The mirror: Nate confronting Dominic's face for the first time
Physical inventory of the apartment — wallet, flip phone, Windows XP desktop, percolator
Nate's muscle memory failures — reaching for a smartphone that doesn't exist
Sarah's morning routine: she calls him Dom, kisses his cheek, notices nothing catastrophic
After Sarah leaves: the weight of irreversibility arriving
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The immediate physical and logistical disorientation of being in a stranger's body in a world twenty years behind the one Nate left
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate understands the scope of his situation — he is Dominic, permanently — and begins to form a survival strategy built on learning the contours of a life he stole.

chapter_spine.conflict: Nate's need to understand his new geography — and the gravitational pull of the strip mall where his father is alive
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate sees Mort alive for the first time in nineteen years. The abstraction of revenge gives way to the embodied reality of a living father he cannot approach.
must_land_beats: Navigating 2005 without GPS — the Rand McNally atlas, the physical strangeness of the pre-smartphone world
The strip mall: first sight of Eastbridge Plaza intact, alive, functioning
Seeing Mort through the window — specific, physical, alive
Nate's paralysis: he cannot go in, cannot speak, cannot be recognized
The realization that 14-year-old Nate exists somewhere in this city
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nate's need to understand his new geography — and the gravitational pull of the strip mall where his father is alive
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate sees Mort alive for the first time in nineteen years. The abstraction of revenge gives way to the embodied reality of a living father he cannot approach.

chapter_spine.conflict: The professional performance demanded by Dominic's job — Nate must fake competence in a field he knows nothing about, in front of a sharp boss who expects results
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate discovers the strip mall project is on Dominic's desk. He is now sitting at the workstation where his family's destruction was administered.
must_land_beats: Arriving at Kassos Development — the office, the converted warehouse, the culture
Meeting Victor: charisma, warmth, the paternal hand on the shoulder
Ray covering for Dominic's strange behavior — confused but loyal
The Eastbridge Plaza file on Dominic's desk: tenant list, demolition timeline, his father's name in black and white
Nate's professional incompetence played for dark comedy — wrong software, wrong terminology, wrong instincts
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The professional performance demanded by Dominic's job — Nate must fake competence in a field he knows nothing about, in front of a sharp boss who expects results
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate discovers the strip mall project is on Dominic's desk. He is now sitting at the workstation where his family's destruction was administered.

chapter_spine.conflict: The intimacy of cohabitation — Sarah knows Dominic's body, habits, and emotional rhythms better than anyone, making her the most dangerous person in Nate's impersonation
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate crosses from performing Dominic's relationship to inhabiting it. The betrayal deepens from practical fraud to moral violation. Sarah may prefer the imposter.
must_land_beats: Domestic rhythms: evenings, meals, the texture of cohabitation
Sarah referencing shared history Nate doesn't have — Providence trip, the fight about his mother, concert tickets
Physical intimacy: the specific wrongness and rightness of sex in a borrowed body — Dominic's body responds in ways Nate doesn't control
Sarah's observation that Dominic has been softer, more present — the cruelest irony
Nate lying awake in a bed that isn't his, next to a woman who loves a dead man
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The intimacy of cohabitation — Sarah knows Dominic's body, habits, and emotional rhythms better than anyone, making her the most dangerous person in Nate's impersonation
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate crosses from performing Dominic's relationship to inhabiting it. The betrayal deepens from practical fraud to moral violation. Sarah may prefer the imposter.

chapter_spine.conflict: Navigating an unfamiliar family's rituals, expectations, and unspoken rules while maintaining the fiction of being their son and brother
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate discovers the Altieri family is warm, messy, and working-class — not the cold corporate clan he imagined. His internal narrative about Dominic-as-villain becomes harder to sustain.
must_land_beats: Renata at the door: face-grabbing, the weight accusation, the overwhelming love
The house: photos on every surface, the smell, the noise — Nate studying family geography
Tommy's arrival: the charm, the borrowed money request, the pattern Nate can see but can't name yet
The dinner itself: overlapping voices, Renata's commentary, the family's practiced chaos
Renata's parting observation: 'You seem different. Good different.' — echoing Sarah's observation, establishing that Nate-as-Dominic is inadvertently a kinder version
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Navigating an unfamiliar family's rituals, expectations, and unspoken rules while maintaining the fiction of being their son and brother
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate discovers the Altieri family is warm, messy, and working-class — not the cold corporate clan he imagined. His internal narrative about Dominic-as-villain becomes harder to sustain.

chapter_spine.conflict: The demolition timeline becoming concrete — not an abstract grievance from the past but a countdown in a file on Nate's desk
chapter_spine.consequence: The strip mall demolition goes from historical event to impending deadline. Nate holds the notification letter bearing Dominic's signature — now his handwriting, his hand.
must_land_beats: Nate alone in the office after hours — the stillness, the fluorescent lights, the desktop whirring
The Eastbridge Plaza file read in full: financial projections, site plans, permits, tenant notifications
The notice letter addressed to Morton Lazar, signed by Dominic Altieri
The demolition date: December 28, 2005 — five months away
The specific horror of holding your father's death warrant in your own hand
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The demolition timeline becoming concrete — not an abstract grievance from the past but a countdown in a file on Nate's desk
chapter_spine.state_shift: The strip mall demolition goes from historical event to impending deadline. Nate holds the notification letter bearing Dominic's signature — now his handwriting, his hand.

chapter_spine.conflict: The gravitational pull of the strip mall community and Nate's growing attachment to people he knows are about to lose everything
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate connects with the community and has his first real conversation with Mort. The demolition stakes shift from historical grievance to present, personal, specific loss.
must_land_beats: Gail's diner: the turkey club, the gossip, the small-world texture of the strip mall community
The barber, the dry cleaner, the bodega — Nate as anonymous regular, a relief from the performance of being Dominic
Mort at the counter: the handshake, the Red Sox conversation, the careful ordinariness
Mort telling a stranger about the redevelopment — 'Nobody asked us'
Nate buying his father's coffee and sitting in the car with shaking hands
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The gravitational pull of the strip mall community and Nate's growing attachment to people he knows are about to lose everything
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate connects with the community and has his first real conversation with Mort. The demolition stakes shift from historical grievance to present, personal, specific loss.

chapter_spine.conflict: Nate's first attempt to save the strip mall from inside collides with his professional incompetence and Victor's institutional power
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate's internal sabotage strategy fails publicly, exposing his incompetence to Victor and straining Ray's protective loyalty. The margin for error in the impersonation shrinks.
must_land_beats: Nate's preparation: studying files, rehearsing arguments, the false confidence of someone who has crammed
The meeting with Victor: Nate's surface-level arguments dismantled one by one
Victor's shift from warmth to clinical assessment — 'Are you feeling all right, Dom?'
Ray's intervention afterward: 'What the hell was that?' — loyalty strained but not broken
Sarah asking about his day, Nate's one-word answer, and the silence that follows
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Nate's first attempt to save the strip mall from inside collides with his professional incompetence and Victor's institutional power
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate's internal sabotage strategy fails publicly, exposing his incompetence to Victor and straining Ray's protective loyalty. The margin for error in the impersonation shrinks.

chapter_spine.conflict: The accumulated gaps in Nate's knowledge of Dominic's life converge — old friends, shared memories, past deals — and the infrastructure of impersonation becomes unsustainable
chapter_spine.consequence: The impersonation reaches critical mass. Nate recognizes that he cannot learn Dominic's history fast enough to prevent exposure. The spiral notebook — his manual for being someone else — is itself an admission of desperation.
must_land_beats: Marco's visit: bluffing through shared memories at a bar, surviving on nods and laughs
Sarah's Cape Cod question: the first time she catches a factual gap rather than a behavioral one
The client call: professional exposure nearly happens, Ray in the doorway saying 'You didn't know who that was'
The spiral notebook: Nate mapping the gaps in his knowledge, building a dossier on the life he's stolen
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The accumulated gaps in Nate's knowledge of Dominic's life converge — old friends, shared memories, past deals — and the infrastructure of impersonation becomes unsustainable
chapter_spine.state_shift: The impersonation reaches critical mass. Nate recognizes that he cannot learn Dominic's history fast enough to prevent exposure. The spiral notebook — his manual for being someone else — is itself an admission of desperation.

chapter_spine.conflict: The moral collapse of Nate's foundational story — the narrative that justified killing Dominic and that has organized Nate's entire adult life
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate discovers Dominic fought the Eastbridge demolition from inside and lost. The man Nate killed was trying to help. The hatred that drove the plot was misdirected.
must_land_beats: Nate navigating Dominic's Outlook Sent folder — the mundane mechanics of the discovery
The email chain: Dominic proposing alternatives, citing tenants by name, including Morton Lazar
Victor's replies: brief, final, the language of institutional closure
Dominic's last email: 'I understand. I'll handle the notifications.' — resignation, not agreement
Nate reading the emails a second time, the specific feeling of having killed a man who was trying to help
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The moral collapse of Nate's foundational story — the narrative that justified killing Dominic and that has organized Nate's entire adult life
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate discovers Dominic fought the Eastbridge demolition from inside and lost. The man Nate killed was trying to help. The hatred that drove the plot was misdirected.

chapter_spine.conflict: Sarah's accumulated observations about the changes in Dominic — months of wrong notes, behavioral shifts, and emotional distance — reaching the point where silence is no longer possible
chapter_spine.consequence: The relationship reaches crisis. Sarah cannot identify the specific truth but she has diagnosed the damage correctly: the man she's living with is fundamentally absent. Nate's deception is exposed as a form of emotional violence even without the literal secret being revealed.
must_land_beats: The trigger: a small domestic failure (wrong drawer) that opens onto months of accumulated evidence
Sarah's catalog of anomalies — precise, specific, devastating in their accuracy
Nate's true-but-useless deflections: 'I'm going through something,' 'I'm not the same person I was'
Sarah's ultimatum: not dramatic but quiet and firm
Sarah on the couch, Nate in the bed, the physical distance as moral verdict
Nate considering — and rejecting — full disclosure
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Sarah's accumulated observations about the changes in Dominic — months of wrong notes, behavioral shifts, and emotional distance — reaching the point where silence is no longer possible

Nate's deception is exposed as a form of emotional violence even without the literal secret being revealed.

chapter_spine.conflict: The double bind of being both the architect of Mort's destruction and the stranger who promised to help — visiting his father's store in the body of the man whose signature is on the demolition order
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate enters the hardware store and makes a promise to his father that may be impossible to keep. The deception is no longer just personal survival — it's now an active relationship built on a lie that Nate cannot sustain or abandon.
must_land_beats: The hardware store interior: sawdust, WD-40, the wall of specialty fasteners — sensory memory colliding with present experience
Buying the level: Nate holding an object that connects him to childhood
Mort's pride in the store — sixteen years, built from nothing, the specificity of his inventory knowledge
Mort describing the redevelopment: not angry, tired — the tone of a man who has accepted he cannot fight the machine
Nate's promise: 'I work in real estate. I could look into it.' — spoken before he can stop himself
Walking out with the level and the promise, both useless, both heavy
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The double bind of being both the architect of Mort's destruction and the stranger who promised to help — visiting his father's store in the body of the man whose signature is on the demolition order
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate enters the hardware store and makes a promise to his father that may be impossible to keep. The deception is no longer just personal survival — it's now an active relationship built on a lie that Nate cannot sustain or abandon.

chapter_spine.conflict: Victor's institutional power and the depth of political corruption behind the demolition — the machine that Dominic tried to fight and that Nate cannot dismantle
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate understands the system Dominic was trapped in. The demolition is not one man's decision but a web of money, politics, and commitments that preceded Dominic and will outlast Nate. Victor's self-description as a cog reframes the entire moral landscape.
must_land_beats: Nate's better-prepared pitch: revised demolition plan, partial preservation, integration of existing businesses
Victor listening seriously — a different quality of attention than in Chapter 9
Victor's explanation: investors, council rezoning, Councilman Dreher's brother-in-law, handshake agreements
Victor's self-description: 'I'm a cog, Dom. A well-paid cog. So are you.'
The implicit threat to Dominic's employment
Nate leaving the meeting understanding the exact trap Dominic was in
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Victor's institutional power and the depth of political corruption behind the demolition — the machine that Dominic tried to fight and that Nate cannot dismantle

chapter_spine.conflict: Tommy's gambling debts producing physical danger, requiring Nate to spend money he cannot afford on obligations that belong to a dead man's family
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate experiences the full weight of Dominic's family responsibilities. The financial cushion is destroyed. A moment of genuine brotherhood passes between Nate and Tommy — inherited, undeserved, and real.
must_land_beats: Tommy at 11 PM: split lip, shaking, the four-thousand-dollar number
The kitchen negotiation: Tommy's desperate optimism, Nate's refusal to enable, the reality of the checking account
Nate channeling Dominic's patterns without meaning to — saying something Dominic would have said
Giving Tommy three thousand dollars, emptying both accounts
Tommy's hug at the door: genuine, heavy, the weight of a brotherhood Nate inherited
Sarah's quiet solidarity afterward — pouring a drink, sitting with him, not saying I told you so
chapter_spine.pressure_source: Tommy's gambling debts producing physical danger, requiring Nate to spend money he cannot afford on obligations that belong to a dead man's family
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate experiences the full weight of Dominic's family responsibilities. The financial cushion is destroyed. A moment of genuine brotherhood passes between Nate and Tommy — inherited, undeserved, and real.

chapter_spine.conflict: The demolition twelve days away, all internal options exhausted, and the crushing recognition that Nate faces the same impossible choice Dominic faced
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate makes the same choice Dominic made — complicity over unemployment — and in doing so completes the novel's central transformation: he has become the man he killed, not through malice but through the same systemic pressure.
must_land_beats: The demolition meeting: timeline finalized, paperwork queued, the machinery in its last phase
Nate alone with the three options: refuse and lose everything, sign and participate, or warn Mort to no effect
Gail reporting at the diner that Mort and Linda are packing the store — the demolition becoming real in other people's bodies
The interior reckoning: Nate thinking about Dominic making this same choice, understanding for the first time that he would have made it too
Signing the paperwork: not dramatic, not anguished — just a man at a desk doing what the job requires
The recognition: he has become Dominic in the way that matters most
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The demolition twelve days away, all internal options exhausted, and the crushing recognition that Nate faces the same impossible choice Dominic faced
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate makes the same choice Dominic made — complicity over unemployment — and in doing so completes the novel's central transformation: he has become the man he killed, not through malice but through the same systemic pressure.

chapter_spine.conflict: The demolition itself — the physical destruction of the place that anchored Mort's life, witnessed by both Mort and the man (Nate/Dominic) whose signature authorized it
chapter_spine.consequence: The defining event of Nate's life happens again with Nate on the other side. The demolition is both a physical fact and the death of Nate's last illusion that he could have changed anything.
must_land_beats: The morning: cold, December, the street blocked off, the small crowd of former tenants and neighbors
Mort and Linda arriving — Mort in his good coat, Linda holding his arm
The demolition itself: efficient, mechanical, dust rising, the diner sign falling
Gail leaving when her sign falls
Mort watching without crying, jaw set, hands in pockets — the specific posture of a man holding himself together
Nate across the street in Dominic's face, carrying Dominic's signature
The machine screw found in the rubble — Mort's specialty stock, a left-hand thread
Driving home, Sarah asking about his day, answering 'Fine'
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The demolition itself — the physical destruction of the place that anchored Mort's life, witnessed by both Mort and the man (Nate/Dominic) whose signature authorized it
chapter_spine.state_shift: The defining event of Nate's life happens again with Nate on the other side. The demolition is both a physical fact and the death of Nate's last illusion that he could have changed anything.

chapter_spine.conflict: The slow accretion of ordinary days in a borrowed life, with the knowledge that Mort is vulnerable and time is running out — not for the demolition, which is past, but for Mort's survival
chapter_spine.consequence: Nate arrives at a new understanding of identity and responsibility — not through epiphany but through the accumulation of small acts of presence. The novel ends with Nate walking into his father's garage, buying finishing nails he doesn't need, answering to a dead man's name.
must_land_beats: Weeks passing: construction on the empty lot, the new development rising, the community dispersing
Nate and Sarah's relationship settling into something real and fragile — not Dominic's relationship, but theirs
Gail's new diner: smaller, two blocks away, the community adapting
Nate visiting Mort's garage — the remaining stock, the space heater, the radio
Nate noticing Mort's quietness, Linda's watchful fear — the signs of a man losing his footing
Nate's quiet campaign: recommending Mort to contractors, showing up weekly, maintaining connection
The final scene: Nate looking at Dominic's hands, parking outside the garage, walking in
Mort's greeting: 'Hey, Dom. What do you need today?'
The finishing nails, the bell on the door, the unresolved ongoing-ness of it
chapter_spine.pressure_source: The slow accretion of ordinary days in a borrowed life, with the knowledge that Mort is vulnerable and time is running out — not for the demolition, which is past, but for Mort's survival
chapter_spine.state_shift: Nate arrives at a new understanding of identity and responsibility — not through epiphany but through the accumulation of small acts of presence. The novel ends with Nate walking into his father's garage, buying finishing nails he doesn't need, answering to a dead man's name.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Changes the subject by asking a question about the other person. If pressed, concedes something small and true to avoid the larger truth. Falls back on vague emotional language: 'I've been going through a lot lately.' The vagueness is deliberate but also reveals his actual inability to articulate what he is.
character_state_table.indirectness: Moderately indirect when performing, very direct in interior monologue. The gap between what he thinks and what he says is the novel's primary engine of irony.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Rarely interrupts others — tends to let people finish and then either agree too quickly or change the subject. When he does interrupt, it's involuntary, triggered by someone saying something that hits close to the truth about his identity.
character_state_table.private_register: Self-deprecating, bitter, occasionally funny in a way that surprises even him. Interior monologue is sharper than speech — Nate is more articulate inside his head than out loud. He narrates his own failures with a clarity that suggests intelligence wasted on resentment. When genuinely moved or afraid, the sarcasm drops and the prose goes simple and sensory.
character_state_table.public_register: Guarded, slightly stiff — the voice of someone performing normalcy in an unfamiliar context. When speaking as Dominic, Nate aims for a warmth that doesn't come naturally to him, producing a slightly off-key friendliness. With strangers he defaults to polite deflection. Under sustained social pressure he retreats into short, noncommittal answers.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Low in speech — Nate is self-conscious about sounding normal and avoids repetition. Higher in thought, where he returns to the same images and formulations compulsively (the mirror, the hands, the signature, the strip mall).
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Frequent in speech, especially when performing as Dominic. Starts a sentence in his own register, catches himself, restarts in what he imagines Dominic would sound like. In interior monologue, self-corrects moral framing: begins a self-justifying thought, then undermines it.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Completes sentences in interior monologue but trails off in speech when he realizes the sentence is leading somewhere dangerous. Restarts after the trail-off rather than picking up where he stopped.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Under low-to-moderate pressure: overthinker, gets stuck in recursive interior loops that delay action. Under acute pressure: goes physically still and vocally flat — the fewer words he uses, the more scared he is. In social emergency (almost caught in the impersonation): becomes abruptly agreeable, says yes to everything, over-smiles. The agreeableness is itself a tell that nobody in 2005 recognizes as such.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Rarely evades — more likely to redirect to the specific thing she wants to talk about. When she does evade, it's by going practical: 'We should eat' or 'I have papers to grade' — removing herself from the emotional space rather than lying about what's in it.
character_state_table.indirectness: Direct about practical matters, moderately indirect about emotional vulnerability. She'll say 'I'm angry' before she'll say 'I'm scared.' She approaches the most difficult things through specific observations rather than grand declarations.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Interrupts when she's excited or when someone is circling a point she's already reached. In arguments, she waits — lets the other person finish, then responds to exactly what they said, which feels more devastating than interruption.
character_state_table.private_register: More vulnerable than her public self suggests. In private she worries aloud, processes through speech rather than silence, and is more openly affectionate. With Dominic/Nate, she's both tender and forensic — she loves him and she's watching him.
character_state_table.public_register: Warm, direct, dry. Teacher voice when she's organizing or explaining — clear, patient, structured. Friend voice is looser, funnier, more willing to be profane. She doesn't perform charm; her warmth is a quality of attention rather than manner.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Low. She makes a point once and expects it to land. If she has to repeat herself, it means the other person wasn't listening, and she treats that as its own problem.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Minimal. Sarah says what she means. When she does revise mid-sentence, it's to be more precise, not to soften.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Almost always completes her sentences. The rare unfinished sentence from Sarah is significant — it means she started to say something she decided mid-breath was too much.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Goes quiet first — the silence is the warning. Then gets very precise, very specific, very calm. She becomes a better arguer under pressure, not a worse one. Tears come after the fight, not during — she refuses to cry as argument.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Answers a different question than the one asked — smoothly, so you don't notice until later. Deflects with anecdotes that seem relevant but redirect the conversation. When truly cornered, simply doesn't respond and moves to the next topic.
character_state_table.indirectness: Highly indirect when threatening. The threat is always framed as concern, advice, or a shared observation about reality. He never says 'I'll fire you' — he says 'I'd hate to see you put yourself in a position where the firm can't support you anymore.'
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Rarely interrupts in social settings — he's a listener who makes you feel heard. In meetings, he'll let someone finish even when he's already decided to reject their proposal. The listening is part of the control.
character_state_table.private_register: Sharper, more economical. When the social performance drops, Victor speaks in shorter sentences and makes fewer gestures. The shift from public to private Victor is subtle — a degree of temperature, not a transformation.
character_state_table.public_register: Avuncular, generous, warm — the voice of a man who builds loyalty through inclusion. Uses 'we' and 'us' constantly. Offers food, drinks, rides home. The warmth is genuine and strategic simultaneously — he doesn't distinguish between liking someone and cultivating them.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Medium. He'll circle back to a point if the listener didn't absorb it the first time, but he frames the repetition as a new thought rather than restating.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Almost never self-corrects. Victor says things once and means them. If he appears to revise, it's usually a rhetorical move — appearing to concede a point to strengthen his actual position.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Always completes his sentences. Measured, unhurried speech. The rare pause is deliberate — for effect, not from uncertainty.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Gets quieter and more still. The warmth doesn't disappear — it recalibrates. Eye contact becomes more sustained. He asks questions he already knows the answer to. The most dangerous Victor is the one who seems most relaxed.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Changes subject to something practical: 'Anyway — you need anything for the house?' The evasion is transparent but effective because people let him have it out of respect.
character_state_table.indirectness: Moderately indirect about his own feelings — talks around pain by talking about practical things. Direct about the physical world: if a tool is wrong for the job, he'll tell you plainly.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Almost never interrupts. Waits. Nods. Lets people finish. The patience is genuine, not strategic.
character_state_table.private_register: Slightly more open but still reserved. Expresses love through action and practical help rather than words. With Linda, communication is largely nonverbal — decades of shared shorthand. When genuinely upset, goes very quiet and physically still.
character_state_table.public_register: Quiet, deliberate, warm. Working-class courtesy — 'sir' and 'ma'am' without stiffness. Talks to customers the way he'd talk to neighbors. Has a storytelling habit: starts with a practical observation and circles to a point you didn't see coming. Not folksy or performed — just a man who pays attention to things.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: He'll say something important once. If the other person didn't hear it, he might rephrase, but he won't belabor.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Occasionally — starts to say something about the demolition or the store, catches himself, redirects to something more neutral. The self-correction is avoidance, not precision.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Completes his sentences. Speaks at a measured pace. The rare trailing off indicates genuine emotion he's choosing not to pursue.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Gets quieter. Works with his hands — sorting, cleaning, organizing — as a way of processing. Doesn't argue or escalate. The stress manifests as withdrawal and physical busyness. When he finally speaks about something painful, it comes out in a single, compressed statement and then he changes the subject.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Doesn't evade much — more likely to just stop talking about something if it's uncomfortable, leaving a conspicuous silence.
character_state_table.indirectness: Mostly direct. When indirect, it's out of discomfort rather than strategy — he'll circle a personal question a couple of times before landing on it.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Interrupts freely in casual conversation — friendly, overlapping, the rhythm of guys who've worked together. In serious conversation, waits but visibly impatient.
character_state_table.private_register: More openly worried when alone with Dominic/Nate. The loyalty is genuine but conditional — he'll cover for strangeness but not for incompetence that threatens his own position.
character_state_table.public_register: Practical, loyal, slightly clipped. Speaks in the shorthand of someone who assumes shared context — references past projects, office politics, and mutual acquaintances without explanation. A workplace friend who operates on proximity and habit rather than deep emotional connection.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: Low. If he's said something once and you haven't responded, he'll wait, but the waiting is pointed.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Completes sentences. Occasional sentence-final particles: 'right?', 'you know?', seeking confirmation.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Gets more direct and more profane. Asks the uncomfortable question head-on rather than circling it. Eye contact intensifies.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Humor. Always humor. Changes the subject by making you laugh. When humor fails, changes the subject by creating a small crisis or drama that redirects attention.
character_state_table.indirectness: Indirect about his problems until forced into directness. Approaches a request by establishing mood, telling a story, building context — and then asking as if it's an afterthought.
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Interrupts constantly in normal conversation — overlapping, jumping in, finishing your sentence wrong. Under real stress, stops interrupting and just listens, which is alarming because it's so unlike him.
character_state_table.private_register: Underneath the charm: anxious, ashamed, genuinely scared when the debts get real. The private Tommy surfaces in flashes — a word that trails off, a silence that goes on too long, the moment after the joke lands and his face hasn't caught up.
character_state_table.public_register: Fast, charming, performatively upbeat. Uses humor to deflect and to establish intimacy simultaneously. Always selling something — a plan, an excuse, a version of events where he comes out OK.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: High. Tommy circles, repeats, rephrases. He's comfortable with redundancy because he's used to making the same pitch in different words until one version lands.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Frequent — starts an excuse, realizes it won't fly, pivots to another excuse in the same breath. The self-correction is tactical, not reflective.
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Often doesn't — thoughts pile up and interrupt each other. Trails off when he realizes he's losing the audience, then restarts from a different angle.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Gets louder and more performative — bigger gestures, faster speech, more jokes. The escalation IS the tell. When truly scared, the performance collapses into silence.

character_state_table.evasion_style: Feeds you. If a topic is too painful, she redirects to the physical: 'Sit. Eat. We'll talk later.' The later never comes and the food is the conversation.
character_state_table.indirectness: Direct about everything except her own worry. She'll tell you your hair looks terrible but won't say 'I'm scared for Tommy.'
character_state_table.interruption_habit: Interrupts constantly and without apology. Conversation with Renata is a series of interruptions punctuated by eating.
character_state_table.private_register: Quieter, more worried. The volume is a performance; underneath she's a woman who has spent decades holding a family together through force of will and cooking.
character_state_table.public_register: Loud, warm, opinionated, constantly feeding people. Comments on weight, appearance, relationships, and life choices with the authority of someone who believes love grants unlimited editorial rights. Speaks in a mix of Italian-American domestic English — not heavy dialect, but inflected with cadence and the occasional Italian word used so naturally it doesn't register as code-switching.
character_state_table.repetition_tolerance: High. She'll say the same thing three different ways in thirty seconds if she thinks you didn't absorb it.
character_state_table.self_correction_tendency: Rare. She says what she means. When she does soften a statement, it's usually by adding food: 'I'm just saying — have more potatoes.'
character_state_table.sentence_completion_style: Runs sentences into each other. One thought becomes the next without clear boundary. Full stops are rare in Renata's speech.
character_state_table.stress_tells: Cooks, cleans, or prays — three forms of the same gesture: doing something with her hands because the problem can't be solved with words. When genuinely frightened (Tommy in danger), goes quiet and still, which terrifies everyone because it's so unlike her.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm for all characters. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or Victor's professional register when delivering serious messages. If a line reads as stiff or literary, check whether contractions would make it sound spoken.

premise: A man accidentally vibe-codes a time machine and travels back to kill his nemesis, only for the machine to be destroyed, leaving him stranded in the past. With no identity, no money, and no way to survive without being exposed, he is forced to assume his nemesis's life. But as he lives in the man's place, he begins to understand how his enemy became who he is.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate chapter energy aggressively. A comic workplace chapter should not be followed by another comic workplace chapter. A grief-heavy scene should be followed by something that lets the reader breathe — not through tonal whiplash but through a genuine shift in the kind of pressure being applied. Vary scene counts, dialogue-to-narration ratios, and temporal compression. Some chapters should cover hours; others should cover weeks. At least three chapters should contain extended quiet: meals, walks, conversations about nothing important. These are not filler — they are the human baseline that makes the high-pressure scenes land.
prose_style_profile.diction: Plain American English with a slightly elevated attention to physical specificity. Not minimalist — the prose has room for interiority and texture — but never ornate. Latinate vocabulary only when the concept demands it. Prefer the concrete noun over the abstract one, the specific tool name over the generic 'tool,' the actual menu item over 'food.' The prose should feel like a smart person thinking clearly, not a writer crafting sentences.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, limited to Nate. The camera sits inside Nate's skull — we see through his eyes, think in his rhythms, and register the world through his specific sensory and emotional biases. The narration should reflect Nate's intelligence and his limitations simultaneously: he notices things (sharp observer) but misinterprets them (blinded by resentment, guilt, and self-pity). The narrative should not explain things Nate wouldn't articulate and should not achieve insights Nate hasn't earned.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied sentence length with a center of gravity around 15-20 words. Short declaratives for landing beats. Longer, accumulative sentences for sensory description and interior processing. Avoid sustained staccato (more than three short sentences in sequence) and sustained lyricism (more than two long, clause-heavy sentences in sequence). The prose should breathe.

theme_spine: Maintain narrative consequence and causal storytelling over tidy resolution.
